did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses deep learning, specifically Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) including ResNet152, DenseNet201, and EfficientNet-B4, to classify dermoscopic images for cutaneous melanoma identification. It evaluates the impact of various activation functions (ReLU, LeakyReLU, ELU, GELU, Swish, Mish, PReLU) on classification accuracy using the ISIC dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental results from a study investigating the influence of activation functions on deep learning models (ResNet152, DenseNet201, EfficientNet-B4) for cutaneous melanoma identification. It includes a hypothesis, methodology involving the ISIC dataset, specific experimental configurations (hardware, hyperparameters), and new quantitative findings (e.g., 90.5% accuracy), rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Influence of activation function in deep learning for cutaneous melanoma identification
brief_summary: This study investigated the influence of various activation functions on the classification performance of pre-trained deep learning models for cutaneous melanoma identification. Using a combined ISIC dataset of 17,114 dermoscopic images, the researchers evaluated ResNet152, DenseNet201, and EfficientNet-B4 architectures with seven different activation functions. The models were trained and tested to determine how activation function selection impacts diagnostic accuracy and other classification metrics. The results indicated that EfficientNet-B4 paired with LeakyReLU achieved the highest accuracy of 90.5%, while DenseNet201 performed best with ReLU at 90.3%. The findings confirm that the choice of activation function significantly affects model performance and that optimal choices are architecture-specific.

lesion_types_studied.details: The primary diagnostic target of the study. The dataset contained 5,192 malignant cases (out of 17,114 total images), classified as 'Malignant' in the binary ground truth labels.
The negative class for the binary classification task. The dataset contained 11,922 benign cases. The study distinguishes these from malignant lesions for diagnostic prediction.

all_task_types.target: Binary diagnostic label (benign vs. malignant melanoma)
all_task_types.details: The study performs binary classification of dermoscopic images to identify cutaneous melanoma. The models predict discrete diagnostic categories (benign or malignant) using pre-trained CNN architectures (ResNet152, DenseNet201, EfficientNet-B4) with various activation functions. Performance was evaluated using classification metrics including accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score on a dataset of 17,114 images.

datasets_used.name: ISIC Combined Dataset
datasets_used.description: A comprehensive dataset composed of 17,114 high-quality dermoscopic images curated from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) challenges conducted in 2018, 2019, and 2020. Images were sourced directly from the official ISIC platform and include structured metadata (ID, gender, age, lesion location) and binary ground truth labels.

use.training: Used for training the ResNet152, DenseNet201, and EfficientNet-B4 models with various activation functions.
use.validation: Used for validation during the training process.
use.testing: Used for final evaluation of model performance (accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score) on a hold-out set of 1,455 images not seen during training.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To enhance the reliability and consistency of the evaluation by mitigating random variation in model performance.
To report the aggregated performance metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score) across the 10 independent test repetitions.

To calculate classification metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score) to address class imbalance in the dataset, ensuring underrepresented classes were appropriately factored into the evaluation.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values or significance tests). It reported mean and standard deviation of performance metrics across 10 independent runs and compared absolute values to draw conclusions about the influence of activation functions.

ISIC Combined Dataset.evaluation_setting: Mean ± Standard Deviation over 10 independent runs
ISIC Combined Dataset.notes: EfficientNet-B4 paired with LeakyReLU achieved the highest overall results across all evaluated metrics. DenseNet201 using ReLU followed closely. For ResNet152, the original model with default configuration slightly outperformed the LeakyReLU-modified version in accuracy (89.2% vs 89.0%) and F1-score (89.4% vs 89.0%). DenseNet201 was noted to be relatively robust to the choice of activation function, with ReLU achieving the highest F1-score (0.903) and accuracy (0.903) among tested options for that architecture.

architecture_used.pretraining: Pre-trained models (ImageNet weights implied by normalization and context of transfer learning)

architecture_details.modification_strategy: Activation functions applied as the final non-linear layer of the pre-trained architectures

training_details.hardware: NVIDIA GeForce RTX 3060 Ti (8GB VRAM), Intel Core i5-10600K, 16GB DDR4 RAM

preprocessing_techniques: Resizing images to architecture-specific dimensions (ranging from 224x224 to 380x380)
Data augmentation including random horizontal and vertical flips
Color jittering to alter image brightness and contrast
Normalization using ImageNet mean [0.485, 0.456, 0.406] and standard deviation [0.229, 0.224, 0.225]

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied to simulate natural variability in skin lesion imaging.
Applied to simulate natural variability in skin lesion imaging.
Applied to account for shifts in camera angle and patient positioning.
Used to alter image brightness and contrast, emulating differences in lighting during image capture.

conclusions: The choice of activation function significantly influences the classification performance of pre-trained deep learning models for cutaneous melanoma identification.
Optimal activation functions are architecture-specific; there is no single universal best activation function across all architectures.
EfficientNet-B4 paired with LeakyReLU achieved the highest overall diagnostic accuracy of 90.5% among the tested configurations.
DenseNet201 achieved its best performance (90.3% accuracy) when paired with the ReLU activation function.

For ResNet152, the model with its default configuration (standard ReLU) slightly outperformed the version modified with LeakyReLU in terms of accuracy (89.2% vs. 89.0%) and F1-score (89.4% vs. 89.0%).
LeakyReLU demonstrated robustness and reliability, achieving the highest classification metrics across most architectures tested in the study.

The study confirms that modifying only the final activation layer of pre-trained CNNs can enhance or maintain diagnostic accuracy, supporting the importance of activation function selection in optimizing melanoma detection pipelines.
important_remarks: The study lacks external validation; it relies exclusively on a combined ISIC dataset (2018-2020) and does not test generalizability to independent, out-of-distribution clinical datasets.

The dataset exhibits significant class imbalance (11,922 benign vs. 5,192 malignant cases), although the authors mitigated this by using weighted averaging for metrics and class weights in the loss function.

The experimental setup is constrained by limited hardware resources (NVIDIA GeForce RTX 3060 Ti with 8GB VRAM), which restricted the batch size for EfficientNet-B4 to 16 and limited the scope of architectures tested.

The study only modifies the final activation layer of pre-trained models, meaning the findings on activation function impact may not generalize to models where activation functions are integrated throughout the architecture.

The paper does not report formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values) to confirm the significance of performance differences between activation functions, relying instead on mean and standard deviation over 10 runs.

The training duration is very short (5 epochs), which may be insufficient for complex activation functions like Mish or GELU to fully converge or demonstrate their theoretical advantages compared to simpler functions like ReLU.
The study focuses on binary classification (benign vs. malignant) and does not address multi-class differentiation among specific types of benign lesions, limiting its clinical granularity.
